items.title: IRGC missiles hit two VLCCs at Fujairah — the Hormuz bypass port, not the strait itself
items.idea: Over the last 48 hours the IRGC fired missiles at two VLCCs near Fujairah, the shuttle-tanker port that lets cargo route around Strait-of-Hormuz risk. HFI Research calls Fujairah "all but dead" as a workaround now, and estimates the escalation removes roughly 6 million b/d of flow from the market — the first attack of its kind on the bypass infrastructure itself, not just Gulf shipping generally.
items.angle: This directly reinforces Goldman's diesel-over-crude hedge thesis circulating the same morning (item 10): if the bypass port is now a target, crude's supposed "escape valves" (rerouting, inventory buffers) just got smaller than the market is pricing.
items.tweetA: Fujairah was the workaround for Hormuz risk — the port where tankers dodge the strait. IRGC just put missiles into two VLCCs there. If the bypass gets shot at, the ~6mbpd of 'redundancy' the market was pricing in doesn't exist anymore.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching Hormuz. The actual tell is Fujairah — the shuttle port that made Hormuz risk hedgeable. Missiles on two VLCCs there is the market losing its backup plan, not just its main pipe.

items.title: Doomberg's contrarian one-liner: blowing up refineries is bearish for crude, not bullish
items.idea: Buried in a Substack Notes teaser, Doomberg flags the counterintuitive mechanism: destroying refining capacity kills a major buyer of crude even as it worsens product (diesel, gasoline) shortages — meaning a refinery strike can push crude and refined products in opposite directions simultaneously.
items.angle: Pairs directly with the Fujairah attack (item 1) and Goldman's diesel-hedge call (item 10) — three sources this morning converging on the same bifurcation: crude has more give than the product complex does.
items.tweetA: Doomberg's contrarian take buried in a teaser: blowing up refineries is bearish for CRUDE. Destroy the demand-conversion machine and you cut oil's biggest buyer, even while diesel and gasoline get scarcer. Crude and product are about to tell very different stories.
items.tweetB: If refineries keep getting hit, don't assume 'more crude bullish.' You may get cheaper crude and pricier diesel at the same time — exactly the bifurcation Goldman's commodities desk is already positioning for.

items.title: Michael Howell: the Gold-Bitcoin liquidity trade "has moved on" — no detail given
items.idea: A one-line teaser from liquidity-cycle economist Michael Howell states the Gold-Bitcoin liquidity pairing — the framework linking both assets to global liquidity expansion — has shifted. No supporting data in the free teaser, but Howell built his entire body of work on global liquidity as the driver of both assets moving together.
items.angle: Worth flagging even without detail: Howell calling a regime shift in a pairing he originated is a bigger tell than either asset's price action this week.
items.tweetA: Michael Howell just flagged that the Gold-Bitcoin liquidity trade 'has moved on' — no detail in the teaser, but Howell built his framework on global liquidity driving both assets together. If he thinks the pairing broke, that's worth more than most people's full theses.
items.tweetB: Gold and Bitcoin have traded as liquidity proxies for two years running. Howell hinting that relationship is shifting is a bigger tell than the price action in either asset this week.

items.title: "A bubble is not when the market falls" — a framework for the week's bubble-callers
items.idea: A bubble isn't a falling market — it's when price permanently decouples from value and is sustained only by the expectation someone else will pay more tomorrow. Since 1928, the S&P 500 has had dozens of 10%+ drawdowns, several 20%+ bear markets and multiple recessions, yet averaged roughly 10%/year with dividends reinvested — real bubbles are rare, corrections are routine.
items.angle: A clean framework to push back with on every "AI bubble popping" headline hitting inboxes this week — distinguishes an ordinary momentum drawdown (see items 8, 15) from an actual price-value break.
items.tweetA: Bubble is not when the market falls. It's when price stops having any relationship to value and the only reason to buy is that someone else will pay more tomorrow. By that definition, most of what's being called 'the AI bubble popping' this week is just a correction.
items.tweetB: S&P 500 since 1928: dozens of 10%+ drawdowns, several 20%+ bear markets, multiple recessions. Average annual return ~10% anyway. If every one of those had been a 'bubble,' the word would mean nothing. Most crashes are corrections wearing a scarier name.

charts.desc: Chart of S&P 500's distance from all-time highs, 1871-2026 (StatOasis) — inside the article

items.title: Microsoft becomes AMD's 4th marquee Helios buyer as Nvidia's 95%+ share gets its first real test
items.idea: Microsoft is deploying AMD's new Helios rack-scale AI system in Azure, joining Meta, OpenAI and Oracle as buyers. Nvidia still controls 95%+ of the data-center GPU market to AMD's ~4.5%, but Futurum's Daniel Newman thinks AMD can reach 20-25% share — "hundreds of billions of dollars of revenue." Helios costs $5-5.5M per rack (Futurum estimate) versus $3.5-4M for Nvidia's Vera Rubin, and AMD's data-center revenue was already up 57% YoY in Q1 2026.
items.angle: The real question per Futurum's own analyst isn't whether Helios is technically as good as Vera Rubin — it's whether AI compute is so supply-constrained that anything sellable gets bought regardless of Nvidia's CUDA moat.
items.tweetA: Microsoft just became AMD's 4th marquee Helios buyer alongside Meta, OpenAI and Oracle. AMD sits at 4.5% of the data-center GPU market vs Nvidia's 95%+. Futurum thinks AMD gets to 20-25% share — that's not a rounding error, that's 'hundreds of billions' in revenue moving.
items.tweetB: The real AMD question isn't 'is Helios as good as Vera Rubin.' It's this, from a Futurum analyst almost verbatim: is AMD winning because it's better, or because capacity is so constrained that whatever you can build, someone will buy? In a supply-constrained market that distinction barely matters for the stock.

charts.desc: 17-minute AMD interview video linked in the piece — worth opening

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Stephen Innes)
items.title: Goldman: Warsh can change how the Fed talks, not what it does
items.idea: Kevin Warsh ordered five Fed task forces covering communications, balance sheet, data interpretation, AI, and inflation understanding. Goldman's David Mericle argues Warsh has real authority to change Fed communications (the SEP, the median dot) but far less room to rebuild the operating machinery without a cautious FOMC's buy-in. AI may eventually prove disinflationary, but Goldman doesn't expect the Fed to ease today on an "uncertain productivity dividend."
items.angle: The market may be pricing a more radical Warsh Fed than Goldman thinks is achievable near-term — a communications overhaul, not a policy revolution.
items.tweetA: Warsh ordered 5 task forces to rewrite how the Fed operates — comms, balance sheet, data, AI, inflation. Goldman's read: he can change how the Fed talks. He can't unilaterally change what it does. If the market is pricing a monetary revolution, this is the reality check.
items.tweetB: The AI-disinflation trade is real in theory, dead in practice for now: Goldman says the Fed won't ease policy today on the strength of an 'uncertain productivity dividend.' Translation — don't expect Warsh to cut rates because AI makes workers more productive. Not yet.

items.title: UK 10-year gilt yield breaks above its Liz Truss-crisis peak on Burnham's PM appointment
items.idea: Andy Burnham became UK PM in a swift, bloodless ascent and named John Healey (no relation to 1970s Chancellor Denis Healey) as Chancellor specifically to reassure bond markets. Gilts sold off anyway: the 10-year yield rose 8.1bps to above 5%, higher than its peak during the actual Truss crisis and the highest since 2008. The UK-German bund spread, near zero at the start of this century, is now roughly 2 percentage points.
items.angle: The market isn't reacting to any specific Burnham policy yet — it's pricing two decades of accumulated distrust in UK fiscal management, the same distrust every new PM's "reassurance" appointment (Blair/BoE 1997, now Burnham/Healey) has failed to fully unwind.
items.tweetA: UK 10-year gilt yield just broke above where it peaked during the Liz Truss crisis — on the appointment of a chancellor whose entire job is reassurance. The market isn't panicking about Burnham's policies yet; it's pricing two decades of accumulated distrust in UK fiscal management.
items.tweetB: Gilts traded roughly level with bunds at the start of this century. That gap is now ~2 percentage points. Every UK PM since has tried a version of the same trick — a 'trust me' appointment (Blair/BoE in '97, now Burnham/Healey). The spread keeps widening anyway.

charts.desc: Gilt yield vs. Truss-era peak chart + gilt-bund spread chart, both embedded in the newsletter

items.title: US semis down 20%+ this month, and China had the exact same momentum surge and reversal
items.idea: The biggest US semiconductor names are down more than 20% this month (a bear market by definition, even up 60%+ over 12 months) — the sharpest "Big Mo" reversal since the pandemic. Bloomberg's Factors-to-Watch shows China saw an equally large momentum run-up and an even nastier July reverse, on completely unrelated stocks.
items.angle: The China parallel is the tell: this isn't an AI-capex-doubt story specific to US chips, it's a global momentum-factor unwind that happened to pick semis as its vehicle — any crowded-trade trigger would have done it.
items.tweetA: US semis down 20%+ this month — bear market by definition, even though they're still up 60% over 12 months. But here's what changes the read: China's momentum trade had an equally big run-up and an even nastier July reversal. This isn't an AI-capex story. It's a global momentum unwind that picked semis as its vehicle.
items.tweetB: Big Mo just had its worst month since the pandemic. When momentum breaks in the US AND China simultaneously, on unrelated stocks, the causal story isn't 'the market changed its mind about AI.' It's 'a crowded factor got too crowded' — and crowded factors don't need a reason to unwind, just a trigger.

charts.desc: Semis 1-month reversal chart + China vs US momentum factor chart — 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Momentum now underperforms the most-shorted-stocks trade under Trump 2.0 — and Europe is the safe harbor
items.idea: Momentum has delivered weaker returns since Trump 2.0 began than simply buying the most heavily shorted stocks while shorting the least shorted — the quant world's polite way of saying trend-followers got run over. Leveraged single-stock ETFs on big semi names handed retail investors the worst possible entry timing. Europe's much lower retail trading volume has made its momentum strategies far more resilient than the US or Asia this month.
items.angle: Leveraged single-stock ETFs are a structural amplifier of momentum crashes that barely existed in past cycles — worth flagging as a durable feature of this cycle specifically, not a one-off.
items.tweetA: Momentum has underperformed 'buy the most shorted, short the least shorted' since Trump 2.0 began. That's the quant world's polite way of saying the trend-following crowd got run over by a squeeze in reverse. Leveraged single-stock semi ETFs handed retail the worst entry of the cycle.
items.tweetB: Want to know why Europe's momentum strategies are holding up better than the US's right now? Not stock selection — retail trading volume. Europe barely has any. No leveraged single-stock ETFs juicing the crowd trade, no violent unwind when it breaks.

charts.desc: Momentum vs. short-interest factor performance chart embedded in the newsletter

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Stephen Innes)
items.title: Goldman's actual Gulf-escalation trade isn't long crude — it's long European diesel timespreads
items.idea: Goldman commodities strategist Daan Struyven argues European diesel timespreads are a cleaner hedge against prolonged Gulf disruption than outright crude length. Crude retains escape valves — rerouted Gulf flows, Chinese demand restraint, inventory buffers. Diesel entered the escalation with already-low OECD inventories, limited refining flexibility, and Russian refinery outages stacking on top.
items.angle: Directly reinforces the Fujairah attack (item 1) and Doomberg's refinery point (item 2) — if the bypass workaround for crude is gone too, Goldman's crude-has-options/diesel-doesn't thesis gets stronger, not weaker.
items.tweetA: Goldman's actual trade recommendation on the Gulf escalation isn't long crude — it's long European diesel timespreads. Their logic: crude has rerouting options and inventory buffers. Diesel, with already-thin OECD stocks and Russian refinery outages stacking up, has none left.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching Brent as the escalation gauge. Goldman's commodities desk is watching diesel timespreads instead, because crude has three ways to route around a Hormuz shock and diesel has zero. The 'real' oil shock might show up in your diesel bill before it shows up at the pump.

items.title: Trump's Jan 1 2027 defense-supply-chain EO names specific covered minerals — and specific tickers
items.idea: Trump's July 20 executive order forces defense contractors to trace every material back to origin and replace suppliers tied to China, Russia, Iran or North Korea starting January 1, 2027, with waivers requiring a documented mitigation plan (not just a claim of non-qualification). Covered materials under 10 U.S.C. §4872: samarium-cobalt and neodymium-iron-boron magnets, tungsten, tantalum, and molybdenum. Copper alone isn't covered — but copper-molybdenum porphyry deposits get a national-security premium via their moly credits.
items.angle: A specific, dated, actionable regulatory catalyst with a real universe of small-caps named — unlike vague "critical minerals onshoring" narratives, this EO draws hard lines around which deposits benefit and which don't.
items.tweetA: Jan 1, 2027 is now a hard date: defense contractors must prove their tungsten, tantalum, molybdenum and rare-earth magnets never touched a China/Russia-linked supply chain, or lose the contract. Copper-only deposits get nothing from this EO — copper-MOLYBDENUM porphyries do. That's a specific distinction most 'onshoring' plays miss.
items.tweetB: The market keeps pricing critical-minerals onshoring as one big theme. This EO says otherwise: a domestic rare-earth mine that ships concentrate to China for separation gets ZERO benefit — value only accrues to projects with the full mine-to-magnet chain onshore or allied. Ownership and processing geography matter more than the ticker's listing venue.

charts.desc: Names FDY, BIG, COPR, NAK, IE (Cu-Mo porphyry) and GMTL, MOON, W (tungsten) as directly affected — worth opening for the full list

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Stephen Innes) — Boom Daily
items.title: SpaceX went from the hottest Nasdaq debut in years to underperforming 80% of comparable IPOs in six weeks
items.idea: SpaceX rose almost 50% from its mid-June IPO, outperforming every comparable Nasdaq debut early on — then reversed to underperform roughly 80% of similarly large Nasdaq IPOs at the same post-listing stage. ETF inflows into SpaceX-linked funds have been stuck between $900M and $1B since late June, suggesting demand arrived in a single burst rather than sustained accumulation. Bloomberg's Simon White models a broader 10-15% market correction to clear the excess.
items.angle: A live case study in how fast a 'hottest IPO in years' narrative flips — SpaceX went from proof of speculative excess to a funding source for shorts in about a month.
items.tweetA: SpaceX rose faster than any comparable Nasdaq IPO in its first weeks, then fell to underperform 80% of them by the same stage. ETF inflows have been stuck at $900M-$1B since late June — the demand arrived in a single burst and never came back. That's not a stock story, that's the entire momentum trade in miniature.
items.tweetB: Bloomberg's Simon White is calling for a 10-15% broader market correction to clear the excess — not a recession call, just a 'the froth needs to go' call. SpaceX is his exhibit A: the same stock that symbolized peak speculative enthusiasm six weeks ago is now the poster child for momentum reversing.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (SpaceX vs. iShares Momentum Factor ETF overlay)

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Stephen Innes) — Boom Daily
items.title: S&P 500 implied correlation near record lows (~7% vs 33% 10-yr average) — funds are betting it snaps back
items.idea: Three-month implied correlation among the S&P 500's largest constituents has fallen to roughly 7%, versus a 10-year average near 33% and 80%+ during the pandemic — the most stock-picker's market in years, priced by an options market that expects almost no shared risk across mega-caps. Adapt Investment Managers is running reverse dispersion (long index vol, short single-stock vol) as a core position betting correlation snaps back, despite it being their worst-performing trade last quarter.
items.angle: A coiled spring for a single macro shock (oil, a Fed surprise, a bad hyperscaler earnings print) to hit every mega-cap at once — the options market is the least prepared it's been in years for exactly that.
items.tweetA: S&P 500 implied correlation just fell to ~7%, versus a 10-year average of 33% and 80%+ during the pandemic. That means options markets are pricing this as the most stock-picker's market in years — and the least prepared for a single shock hitting everything at once.
items.tweetB: There's a hedge fund actively betting correlation snaps back — reverse dispersion, long index vol, short single-stock vol — even after it was their worst trade last quarter. When smart money keeps buying insurance after losing money on it, that's not conviction, that's a read on how cheap that insurance has gotten.

charts.desc: Implied correlation vs. 10-yr average chart — 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Stephen Innes) — Boom Daily, citing UBS
items.title: US median wealth per adult: $69k, ranked 28th globally — between Austria and Greece
items.idea: UBS's Arend Kapteyn: the US holds 36% of global personal wealth, ~40% of the world's millionaires, nearly a third of its billionaires, and added roughly 1,200 new dollar millionaires per day in 2025. Yet median wealth per US adult is only $69,000 — 28th globally, between Austria (~$71k) and Greece (~$59k). On average wealth the US ranks 2nd globally at ~$700k, behind only Switzerland (~$910k); Luxembourg tops median wealth at $394k.
items.angle: The average/median gap is itself the explanation for why consumer sentiment stays weak even as headline indices sit near record highs — most of the 'wealth' isn't distributed anywhere near the middle of the population.
items.tweetA: The US has 36% of global wealth and adds 1,200 new millionaires a day. Median wealth per US adult? $69,000 — 28th in the world, sandwiched between Austria and Greece. Same country, same year, two completely different stories depending on whether you use average or median.
items.tweetB: Luxembourg's median adult is worth $394k. The median American is worth $69k. But average American wealth is $700k, 2nd in the world. That average/median gap IS the explanation for why consumer sentiment stays weak while the S&P sits near record highs — most of the 'wealth' isn't distributed anywhere near the median.

charts.desc: UBS median vs. average wealth-by-country ranking chart

items.title: Bitcoin-equity correlation hits a record 0.96 — Alphabet, Tesla and Intel earnings now decide crypto's week
items.idea: Bitcoin's correlation with equities sits at a record 0.96. Because of that, this week's Alphabet (Tue), Tesla (Wed) and Intel (Thu) earnings — read for whether the AI capex boom is still climbing — will drive crypto's direction more than the Fed or the CLARITY Act, per Tiger Capital Research's Monday roundup.
items.angle: Crypto has stopped functioning as a diversifier and become a leveraged AI-capex derivative — a positioning risk for anyone still holding BTC as ballast against AI-stock drawdowns.
items.tweetA: BTC-equity correlation just hit a record 0.96. That means this week's actual Bitcoin catalyst isn't the Fed, isn't CLARITY — it's Alphabet, Tesla and Intel earnings. If you're holding Bitcoin as a diversifier from AI-stock risk right now, you're holding a levered AI-stock position with extra steps.
items.tweetB: 0.96 correlation to equities is basically 'Bitcoin is a semiconductor stock now.' The crypto-native catalysts everyone's debating — CLARITY Act, ETF flows — matter less this week than whether Alphabet and Tesla beat estimates. That's a strange place for the 'digital gold' thesis to be standing.

items.title: Brent round-trips from a 1-month high near $91 to WTI down $3 on a single ceasefire headline
items.idea: Brent spiked as much as 4% toward $90-91 (highest since June) as US-Iran strikes widened beyond military targets, then WTI fell roughly $3 to $80-81 within the same session after Reuters reported Iranian mediators proposing a 10-day ceasefire to revive the earlier interim US-Iran deal. That's the same inflation risk that had eased on this month's soft US CPI now swinging violently in the other direction.
items.angle: A single wire headline erased a ~$10/bbl move — the "disinflation is back" narrative built on soft July CPI is really a bet that a 10-day ceasefire holds, a much shakier foundation than a data print.
items.tweetA: Brent went from a 1-month high near $91 to WTI down $3 to the low $80s within the same session, on a single Reuters ceasefire headline. The 'disinflation is back' narrative built on this month's soft CPI is really a bet that a 10-day ceasefire holds. That's a much shakier foundation than a CPI print.
items.tweetB: Same war, same week, $10 oil swing on one wire headline. If Iran's ceasefire proposal falls apart, July's CPI print reverses everything June's soft numbers bought the Fed. The July 28-29 FOMC is now effectively a bet on whether a 10-day truce holds.

items.title: Bitcoin ETF inflows resume but are "peanuts" against ~$5.4B in 2026 outflows — Standard Chartered $100k vs Galaxy $40-46k
items.idea: Bitcoin ETF inflows have resumed but remain "peanuts" relative to the roughly $5.4B in 2026 outflows and June's record redemption month. The outlook is a genuine two-sided split: Standard Chartered renewed its $100,000 year-end target and argues BTC may be near a cycle bottom, while Galaxy Research warns of downside toward $40,000-$46,000 and flags that buyers who paid $75,000-$126,000 are now realizing losses. Polymarket splits the difference at $70,000-$75,000.
items.angle: The flow data, not the price targets, is the tell: real demand recovery requires inflows to grow well beyond "peanuts," and neither bull nor bear case has that confirmation yet.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin ETFs are seeing inflows again — but they're 'peanuts' against $5.4B in 2026 outflows and June's record redemption month. Standard Chartered says $100k by year-end. Galaxy Research says $40-46k downside. Nobody has the flow data to back either call yet.
items.tweetB: The most useful number in the whole BTC debate isn't a price target, it's the flow-to-outflow ratio: inflows resumed but stayed 'peanuts' relative to the exodus. Until that changes, every $100k or $40k call is a story, not a signal.

items.title: A quantum-recovery tool for Bitcoin wallets now runs in 243ms on a laptop — but not for Satoshi's 1.1M coins
items.idea: Project Eleven funded a proof letting a wallet's key-derivation path stand in as proof of ownership after quantum computers become capable of forging signatures — the recovery process runs in 243 milliseconds on a laptop. It explicitly does not cover Satoshi's roughly 1.1 million dormant coins. Michael Saylor separately called a proposed plan to "clean up the blockchain" (reclaiming lost/dormant coins) "a bad idea," reigniting Bitcoin's governance debate.
items.angle: The quantum-forgery threat to Bitcoin just went from theoretical to having a working 243ms recovery tool — worth tracking as an actual timeline forming around a risk most crypto investors still treat as decades away.
items.tweetA: There's now a working tool that recovers a Bitcoin wallet's ownership proof after a quantum computer forges its signature — in 243 milliseconds, on a laptop. The 'quantum risk is decades away' assumption just got a concrete counter-example. It still doesn't cover Satoshi's 1.1 million coins, which is its own governance landmine.
items.tweetB: Saylor calling the plan to 'clean up the blockchain' a bad idea is really a fight about whether Bitcoin's supply is actually fixed once quantum-vulnerable coins can be identified and reclaimed. That's a much bigger philosophical fight than BIP-110 ever was, and it's just getting started.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Stephen Innes)
items.title: Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid: the real AI risk is a US capex "overcycle," not a demand collapse
items.idea: DB's Jim Reid frames the central AI market risk as a capex overcycle: Chinese open-weight models are challenging the assumption that frontier-level intelligence must be expensive, closed and capital-intensive. If comparable performance can be delivered with fewer chips, less energy and less capital, today's biggest builders may not earn the returns embedded in their valuations, even as the tech selloff broadens from software into semiconductors and the wider AI complex.
items.angle: Pairs directly with the AMD/Nvidia cost-per-token pitch (item 5) and Tiger Capital's reference to Friday's Kimi shock (item 15-18 window) — three separate sources this morning circling the same risk without naming it together: the AI trade is quietly repricing around cost-per-token, not raw capability.
items.tweetA: Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid just wrote the sentence AI capex bulls should be scared of: the risk isn't that AI doesn't work, it's a 'CapEx overcycle' — spending hundreds of billions on chips and power that turn out to be more than the workload needed, because Chinese open-weight models are proving you can get comparable output with far less.
items.tweetB: Three different newsletters this morning circle the same risk without naming it together: DB's Jim Reid on cheaper Chinese models cracking US AI economics, Tiger Capital flagging Friday's Kimi shock rattling chips, and AMD's Helios pitch literally being about 'lowest cost per token.' The AI trade is quietly repricing around cost-per-token, not raw capability.
